Article.ArticleTitle: Relative contribution of dark carbon fixation to total carbon uptake along a latitudinal transect in the Seychelles-Chagos thermocline ridge, western Indian ocean.

Abstract.AbstractText: Dark carbon fixation (DCF) represents a significant but still poorly constrained component of marine carbon cycling, particularly in tropical open-ocean systems. Here, we investigated spatial variability of photosynthetic primary production and DCF along a meridional transect (20°S to 2°N, 65°E) in the western Indian Ocean. Using the 13C tracer incubations, carbon fixation rates were quantified from surface to 800 m depth at eight stations during May-June 2023. Depth-integrated primary production ranged from 13.8 to 283.8 mg C m-2 d-1. In contrast, depth-integrated DCF ranged from 3.1 to 41.3 mg C m-2 d-1 in the euphotic zone and 24.3 to 127.8 mg C m-2 d-1 when integrated over the full 0-800 m water column. DCF was consistently detectable throughout the water column and exhibited pronounced spatial variability across the transect. The relative contribution of DCF to primary production varied widely, with DCF-to-primary-production ratios ranging from 32 to 527% (0-800 m). In oligotrophic regions, depth-integrated DCF frequently equaled or exceeded euphotic-zone primary production, whereas in more productive, it represented a smaller but still substantial fraction (32-44%) of total carbon fixation. Correlation analyses indicated that DCF was closely associated with organic matter availability, oxygenated conditions, and reduced substrates generated through remineralization, whereas primary production exhibited weaker and more variable relationships with individual environmental variables. These findings demonstrate that biologically mediated carbon fixation occurs throughout the water column in the western Indian Ocean and that restricting assessments to euphotic-zone primary production can substantially underestimate total biological carbon fixation in oligotrophic ocean environments.
